question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an AddressBookQuery with no authentication header to the getNodes() gRPC endpoint, given that no authentication or authorization check is visible in the controller, triggering unrestricted access to the full node address book, causing exposure of all network node topology including IP addresses, ports, and certificate hashes to any unauthenticated caller?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an AddressBookQuery with limit set to Integer.MAX_VALUE (2147483647), given that AddressBookFilter only enforces @Min(0) with no @Max constraint, triggering networkService.getNodes() to stream an unbounded result set, causing server-side memory exhaustion and denial of service for all other clients?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an AddressBookQuery with limit=0, given that @Min(0) allows zero as a valid value and no explicit handling of zero-limit exists in toFilter(), triggering networkService.getNodes() to return all entries without any pagination cap, causing unbounded data exfiltration and resource exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/NetworkController.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user open a getNodes() stream and immediately cancel it in a tight loop, given that the cancel handler only calls disposable::dispose with no rate limiting on reconnection, triggering repeated subscription and disposal cycles on the reactive pipeline, causing CPU exhaustion and degraded service availability?
